content.text:
## Base Configuration File for Spring Boot Template API
# ==========================
# This file contains the base configuration for the Spring Boot Template API.
# It includes settings for server, application, database, JPA, and JWT.
# ==========================
# Note: This file is intended to be used as a template. Modify the values as needed for your environment.
# ==========================


# Spring Boot Configuration
server.port=8080
server.servlet.context-path=/api/v1/

# Application Configuration
application.title=Spring Template
application.version=2.0

# JPA Configuration
spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto=update

# JWT Configuration
security.jwt.secret-key=34743777217A25432A462D4A614E645267556B58703273357638782F413F4428472B4B6250655368566D597133743677397A244226452948404D635166546A57
security.jwt.authorization-key=614E645267556B58703273357638792F423F4528482B4B6250655368566D597133743677397A24432646294A404E635166546A576E5A7234753778214125442A
security.jwt.expiration=86400000
security.jwt.refresh-expiration=604800000
security.jwt.issuer=SpringTemplate

# Rate Limit Configuration
ratelimit.enabled=true
ratelimit.limit=100
ratelimit.duration-minutes=1

# Rate Limit Category Configuration
ratelimit.strict.limit=10
ratelimit.strict.duration-minutes=1
ratelimit.standard.limit=60
ratelimit.standard.duration-minutes=1
ratelimit.relaxed.limit=200
ratelimit.relaxed.duration-minutes=1
ratelimit.authentication.limit=5
ratelimit.authentication.duration-minutes=1

# Redis Configuration
spring.data.redis.host=redis
spring.data.redis.port=6379

# Spring Session with Redis
spring.session.store-type=redis
spring.session.redis.namespace=spring:session
spring.session.timeout=30m

# Kafka Configuration
spring.kafka.bootstrap-servers=kafka:9092
spring.kafka.consumer.group-id=spring-template-group
spring.kafka.consumer.auto-offset-reset=earliest
spring.kafka.consumer.key-deserializer=org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer
spring.kafka.consumer.value-deserializer=org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer
spring.kafka.producer.key-serializer=org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer
spring.kafka.producer.value-serializer=org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer

# Kafka Logging Configuration
logging.level.org.apache.kafka=INFO
logging.level.org.springframework.kafka=INFO
# To enable debug logging for troubleshooting, use DEBUG instead of INFO
# logging.level.org.apache.kafka=DEBUG
# logging.level.org.springframework.kafka=DEBUG

# Actuator Configuration
management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info,metrics
management.endpoint.health.show-details=when-authorized
management.endpoint.health.probes.enabled=true

# Spring Boot Template

A production-ready template for Spring Boot projects with modern best practices and built-in infrastructure.

![Docker Compose](https://imgur.com/qWLYDrg.png)
![Swagger UI](https://imgur.com/5MixfZe.png)

## Features

- **Spring Boot 4.0.0** (Java 25)
- **UUID Primary Keys** via Hibernate `@UuidGenerator`
- **JWT Authentication & Authorization** with refresh tokens
- **Role-based Security** with Spring Security
- **Spring Boot Actuator** for health, info, and metrics endpoints
- **Request Correlation ID** — automatic `X-Correlation-ID` header propagation and MDC logging
- **Structured JSON Logging** — human-readable console + JSON file output (Logstash format)
- **Pagination** — built into `BaseService` and controllers via Spring Data `Pageable`
- **Rate Limiting** — annotation-based (`@RateLimit`, `@RateLimitCategory`) with configurable categories
- **Idempotency** — `@Idempotent` annotation with Redis-backed request deduplication
- **Swagger UI** for API documentation ([http://localhost:8080/api/v1/swagger-ui/index.html](http://localhost:8080/api/v1/swagger-ui/index.html))
- **PostgreSQL**, **Redis**, **Kafka & Zookeeper** — all containerized via Docker Compose
- **Multi-stage Dockerfile** with dependency caching and JRE runtime
- **JaCoCo Code Coverage** reports
- **Maven Enforcer** — ensures Java 25+ and Maven 3.8.7+
- **Lombok** and **MapStruct** for reduced boilerplate
- **Environment Isolation** — run dev, test, and prod in parallel via Docker Compose
- **Unit & Integration Tests** for services, controllers, and infrastructure

> **Note:** Java 25 and Maven 3.8.7+ are required.

---

## Quick Start

### 1. Use This Template

Click **"Use this template"** on GitHub, then clone your new repository:

```sh
git clone https://github.com/<your-username>/<your-repo>.git
cd <your-repo>
```

### 2. Initialize Your Project

Run the initialization script to rename packages, modules, and containers to match your project:

```sh
./init-project.sh --group-id com.acme --artifact my-app --name "My App" --author "Your Name"
```

| Option | Description | Required |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| `--group-id` | Maven groupId / Java package root (e.g. `com.acme`) | Yes |
| `--artifact` | Artifact name in kebab-case (e.g. `my-app`) | Yes |
| `--name` | Human-readable project name (default: derived from artifact) | No |
| `--author` | Author name for `@author` tags (default: `tzesh`) | No |

You can also run it interactively — omit the flags and you'll be prompted:

```sh
./init-project.sh
```

The script will:
- Rename Java packages (`com.tzesh.springtemplate` -> your package)
- Rename module directories (`spring-boot-template-api` -> `<artifact>-api`, etc.)
- Update Docker container names, Kafka group IDs, and all references
- Replace `@author` tags if `--author` is provided
- Self-delete after completion

### 3. Verify the setup

```sh
./mvnw clean install
```

### 4. Start infrastructure and run

```sh
# Start PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka
docker compose up -d db redis zookeeper kafka

# Run the application
./mvnw -pl spring-boot-template-api spring-boot:run
```

Or run everything with Docker:

```sh
docker compose up -d
```

The application will be available at [http://localhost:8080/api/v1/swagger-ui/index.html](http://localhost:8080/api/v1/swagger-ui/index.html).

---

## Project Structure

```
SpringBootTemplate/
├── pom.xml                          # Parent POM (enforcer, JaCoCo, shared deps)
├── spring-boot-template-core/       # Core module (base classes, annotations, exceptions)
│   └── src/main/java/.../base/
│       ├── annotation/              # @RateLimit, @Idempotent, etc.
│       ├── dto/                     # BaseDTO
│       ├── entity/                  # BaseEntity
│       ├── exception/               # NotFoundException, RateLimitExceededException, etc.
│       ├── handler/                 # @ControllerAdvice exception handlers
│       ├── mapper/                  # BaseMapper (MapStruct)
│       ├── response/                # BaseResponse wrapper
│       └── service/                 # BaseService (CRUD + pagination)
├── spring-boot-template-api/        # API module (controllers, services, config)
│   ├── Dockerfile                   # Multi-stage build
│   └── src/main/java/.../
│       ├── config/
│       │   ├── correlation/         # CorrelationIdFilter
│       │   ├── idempotency/         # IdempotencyAspect
│       │   ├── ratelimit/           # RateLimitAspect, RateLimitingFilter
│       │   └── security/            # SecurityChain, JWT filters
│       ├── controller/              # REST controllers
│       └── service/                 # Business services
├── docker-compose.yml               # PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, app
├── init-project.sh                  # Project initialization script
├── .editorconfig                    # Editor formatting rules
├── .gitattributes                   # Git line ending config
└── .dockerignore                    # Docker build exclusions
```

---

## API Usage

1. **Register** a new user via `POST /auth/register`
2. **Login** via `POST /auth/login` to obtain a JWT token
3. **Authorize** in Swagger UI using the `Authorize` button
4. **Access** secured endpoints (e.g. user management requires `ADMIN` role)

---

## Actuator Endpoints

Health, info, and metrics are exposed at:

| Endpoint | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `GET /api/v1/actuator/health` | Application health status |
| `GET /api/v1/actuator/info` | Application info |
| `GET /api/v1/actuator/metrics` | Application metrics |

Health details are shown when authenticated with an authorized role. Kubernetes liveness and readiness probes are enabled.

---

## Correlation ID

Every request is assigned a correlation ID for distributed tracing:

- If the client sends an `X-Correlation-ID` header, it is reused
- Otherwise, a UUID is generated automatically
- The correlation ID is added to the MDC for all log output and returned in the response header

---

## Rate Limiting

### Annotation-based (per-endpoint)

```java
@RateLimit(limit = 20, duration = 1, key = RateLimitKeyStrategy.USER)
@RateLimitCategory(RateLimitCategoryType.STANDARD)
```

### Configurable categories in `application.properties`

| Category | Default Limit | Duration |
|----------|--------------|----------|
| `strict` | 10 req | 1 min |
| `standard` | 60 req | 1 min |
| `relaxed` | 200 req | 1 min |
| `authentication` | 5 req | 1 min |

---

## Idempotency

Annotate mutating endpoints with `@Idempotent` to prevent duplicate processing. Clients must send an `Idempotency-Key` header. Backed by Redis.

```java
@PostMapping("/")
@Idempotent
public ResponseEntity<BaseResponse<UserDTO>> createUser(...) { ... }
```

---

## Kafka Messaging

- Kafka and Zookeeper are included in Docker Compose
- `POST /kafka/send` sends messages to Kafka (supports optional scheduled delivery)
- Consumer logs received messages
- Logging level configurable in `application.properties`

---

## Environment Configuration

Use `.env` files for environment-specific variables and Docker Compose override files for port/resource separation:

```sh
# Run test environment
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.test.override.yml --env-file .env.test -p myapp_test up -d

# Run prod environment
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.override.yml --env-file .env.prod -p myapp_prod up -d
```

---

## Code Coverage

JaCoCo reports are generated during `mvn test` and available at:

```
spring-boot-template-api/target/site/jacoco/index.html
spring-boot-template-core/target/site/jacoco/index.html
```

---

## Contributing

See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for setup instructions, code style, and PR process.

---

## License

See [LICENSE](LICENSE) for details.

data.content:
Here is a comprehensive explanation of how this Spring Boot template handles security, JWT authentication, role-based access, and how users interact with secured endpoints.

---

## 1. High-Level Architecture Overview

The template implements a **stateless JWT-based authentication and authorization** system built on top of Spring Security. There is no server-side session; instead, every authenticated request carries a signed JSON Web Token in the `Authorization` header. The security pipeline spans these layers:

| Layer | Key Classes |
|-------|-------------|
| **Filter chain** | `SecurityChain`, `AuthenticationFilter` |
| **JWT operations** | `JwtService` (generate, validate, parse) |
| **Authentication flow** | `AuthenticationService`, `AuthenticationController` |
| **Token persistence / revocation** | `TokenEntity`, `TokenRepository`, `LogoutService` |
| **User loading** | `SecurityServices` (provides `UserDetailsService`, `AuthenticationProvider`, `PasswordEncoder`) |
| **Error handling** | `AuthenticationEntryPoint`, `AccessDeniedExceptionHandler` |
| **Roles & Permissions** | `Role` enum, `Permission` enum |

---

## 2. JWT Token Lifecycle

### 2.1 Token Generation (`JwtService`)

The `JwtService` is responsible for creating and validating tokens. It uses the **jjwt** library with HMAC-SHA signing.

- **Signing key**: A Base64-encoded secret read from `${security.jwt.secret-key}` in `application.properties`.
- **Issuer**: Configured via `${security.jwt.issuer}` (defaults to `SpringTemplate`).
- **Access token expiration**: `${security.jwt.expiration}` = `86400000` ms (24 hours).
- **Refresh token expiration**: `${security.jwt.refresh-expiration}` = `604800000` ms (7 days).

The token payload contains:
- `sub` — the username
- `iat` — issued-at timestamp
- `exp` — expiration timestamp
- `iss` — issuer claim

```java
// From JwtService.buildToken()
Jwts.builder()
    .claims(extraClaims)
    .subject(userDetails.getUsername())
    .issuedAt(new Date(System.currentTimeMillis()))
    .expiration(new Date(System.currentTimeMillis() + expiration))
    .issuer(issuer)
    .signWith(getSignInKey())
    .compact();
```

### 2.2 Token Validation

When a request arrives, `JwtService.isTokenValid()` checks two conditions:
1. The username extracted from the token matches the `UserDetails` username.
2. The token has not expired (the `exp` claim is in the future).

---

## 3. Authentication Filter Pipeline (`AuthenticationFilter`)

The `AuthenticationFilter` extends `OncePerRequestFilter` and runs on every request *before* Spring's `UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter`. Its logic:

1. **Skip auth paths**: If the request path contains `/api/v1/auth`, the filter passes through immediately (login/register/refresh are public).

2. **Extract the JWT**: Reads the `Authorization` header. If it is missing or does not start with `Bearer `, the request proceeds unauthenticated (and will be rejected later if the endpoint requires authentication).

3. **Parse the username**: `jwtService.extractUsername(jwt)` decodes the `sub` claim.

4. **Load user details**: If the username is non-null and no authentication is already in the `SecurityContext`, the filter loads the full `UserDetails` from the database via `UserDetailsService`.

5. **Check token is still valid in the database**: It queries `TokenRepository.findByToken(jwt)` and verifies the stored `TokenEntity` is neither expired nor revoked. This is the mechanism for **token revocation** — once a token is marked revoked/expired in the database, it will be rejected even if the JWT signature and expiration are still valid.

6. **Set the SecurityContext**: If both the JWT validation and the database check pass, a `UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken` is created with the user's authorities (roles + permissions) and placed into the `SecurityContextHolder`.

```java
// Key validation step in AuthenticationFilter
var isTokenValid = tokenRepository.findByToken(jwt)
    .map(t -> !t.isExpired() && !t.isRevoked())
    .orElse(false);

if (jwtService.isTokenValid(jwt, userDetails) && isTokenValid) {
    UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken authToken = new UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken(
        userDetails, null, userDetails.getAuthorities()
    );
    authToken.setDetails(new WebAuthenticationDetailsSource().buildDetails(request));
    SecurityContextHolder.getContext().setAuthentication(authToken);
}
```

---

## 4. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

### 4.1 Roles

The `Role` enum defines three roles with escalating privileges:

| Role | Permissions |
|------|------------|
| **USER** | None (empty set) — basic authenticated user |
| **MANAGER** | `MANAGER_READ`, `MANAGER_UPDATE`, `MANAGER_DELETE`, `MANAGER_CREATE` |
| **ADMIN** | All 8 permissions — both `ADMIN_*` and `MANAGER_*` |

### 4.2 Permissions

The `Permission` enum defines granular authorities:

```java
ADMIN_READ("admin:read"),
ADMIN_UPDATE("admin:update"),
ADMIN_CREATE("admin:create"),
ADMIN_DELETE("admin:delete"),
MANAGER_READ("management:read"),
MANAGER_UPDATE("management:update"),
MANAGER_CREATE("management:create"),
MANAGER_DELETE("management:delete");
```

Each role's `getAuthorities()` method converts permissions into `SimpleGrantedAuthority` objects and also adds a `ROLE_<name>` authority (e.g., `ROLE_ADMIN`). This enables both role-based and authority-based checks.

### 4.3 URL-Level Authorization (`SecurityChain`)

The `SecurityFilterChain` bean in `SecurityChain` configures which URLs require which roles/authorities:

```java
.authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth
    // Public endpoints (no auth required)
    .requestMatchers(
        "/auth/**",           // Login, register, refresh
        "/v3/api-docs/**",   // OpenAPI docs
        "/swagger-ui/**",     // Swagger UI
        "/actuator/**"        // Health, metrics
    ).permitAll()

    // /management/** requires ADMIN or MANAGER role
    .requestMatchers("/management/**").hasAnyRole(ADMIN.name(), MANAGER.name())

    // Fine-grained method + authority checks on /management/**
    .requestMatchers(GET,    "/management/**").hasAnyAuthority(ADMIN_READ, MANAGER_READ)
    .requestMatchers(POST,   "/management/**").hasAnyAuthority(ADMIN_CREATE, MANAGER_CREATE)
    .requestMatchers(PUT,    "/management/**").hasAnyAuthority(ADMIN_UPDATE, MANAGER_UPDATE)
    .requestMatchers(PATCH,  "/management/**").hasAnyAuthority(ADMIN_UPDATE, MANAGER_UPDATE)
    .requestMatchers(DELETE, "/management/**").hasAnyAuthority(ADMIN_DELETE, MANAGER_DELETE)

    // Everything else requires authentication (any role)
    .anyRequest().authenticated()
)
```

### 4.4 Method-Level Authorization (`@PreAuthorize`)

In addition to URL rules, individual controller methods use Spring's `@PreAuthorize` annotation for fine-grained access:

```java
// UserController — only ADMIN can create users
@PostMapping("/")
@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")
public ResponseEntity<BaseResponse<UserDTO>> createUser(...) { ... }

// AuthenticationController — authorize endpoint for ADMIN/USER/MODERATOR
@PostMapping("/authorize")
@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN') || hasRole('USER') || hasRole('MODERATOR')")
public ResponseEntity<BaseResponse<AuthenticationResponse>> authorize(...) { ... }
```

This means the template enforces security at **two levels**: the HTTP filter chain (URL patterns) and individual method annotations.

---

## 5. How Users Interact with Secured Endpoints

The expected user flow, as documented in the README:

### Step 1: Register
```
POST /api/v1/auth/register
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "username": "johndoe",
  "email": "john@example.com",
  "name": "John Doe",
  "password": "securePassword123"
}
```
**Response**: An `AuthenticationResponse` containing `accessToken` and `refreshToken`. The newly registered user gets `Role.USER` by default.

### Step 2: Login
```
POST /api/v1/auth/login
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "username": "johndoe",
  "password": "securePassword123"
}
```
**Response**: A fresh `accessToken` + `refreshToken`. On login, all previous tokens for that user are **revoked** (single-session behavior).

### Step 3: Use the Token
For all subsequent requests to protected endpoints, users must include the JWT in the `Authorization` header:
```
Authorization: Bearer <accessToken>
```

### Step 4: Refresh Expired Tokens
When the access token expires (after 24 hours), users can get a new one:
```
POST /api/v1/auth/refresh-token
Authorization: Bearer <refreshToken>
```
This returns a new `accessToken` while the `refreshToken` remains valid for 7 days.

### Step 5: Logout
```
POST /api/v1/auth/logout
Authorization: Bearer <accessToken>
```
The `LogoutService` finds the token in the database, marks it as `expired=true` and `revoked=true`, then clears the `SecurityContext`.

### Step 6: Role Escalation (Authorization)
To grant a user elevated privileges (e.g., promote to ADMIN), an administrator calls:
```
POST /api/v1/auth/authorize
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "username": "johndoe",
  "role": "ADMIN",
  "secret": "<authorization-key>"
}
```
The `authorization-key` is a shared secret configured in `application.properties` (`security.jwt.authorization-key`). This changes the user's role in the database. The user must then re-login to get a new token with updated authorities.

### Step 7: Accessing Admin/Management Endpoints
With an `ADMIN` role, the user can now call endpoints like:
```
GET    /api/v1/users/          — List all users
POST   /api/v1/users/          — Create a user
DELETE /api/v1/users/{id}      — Delete a user
PATCH  /api/v1/users/{id}      — Update a user
```
A `USER`-role token would receive a **403 Forbidden** on these endpoints.

---

## 6. Additional Security Features

### 6.1 Session Management
Sessions are completely **stateless** (`SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS`). No `JSESSIONID` cookie is used. Every request is authenticated solely by its JWT.

### 6.2 CSRF Protection
CSRF is **disabled** — appropriate for a stateless REST API that does not use cookies for authentication.

### 6.3 Password Storage
Passwords are encoded using **BCrypt** (`BCryptPasswordEncoder`), configured in `SecurityServices`.

### 6.4 Unauthorized / Access Denied Responses
- **`AuthenticationEntryPoint`**: Returns a structured JSON 401 response with details when a request lacks valid credentials.
- **`AccessDeniedExceptionHandler`** (in the core module): Returns a structured JSON 403 response when an authenticated user lacks the required role/authority.

### 6.5 Token Revocation
The `TokenEntity` / `TokenRepository` pattern provides a **token blocklist**. On login, all previous tokens are revoked. On logout, the current token is revoked. The `AuthenticationFilter` checks this blocklist on every request.

### 6.6 Rate Limiting on Auth Endpoints
The `AuthenticationController` is annotated with `@RateLimitCategory(RateLimitCategoryType.AUTHENTICATION)`, which limits authentication endpoints to **5 requests per minute** (configurable). This mitigates brute-force attacks on login.

### 6.7 Idempotency on Registration
The register endpoint is annotated with `@Idempotent(ttl = 1, timeUnit = TimeUnit.HOURS)`, backed by Redis, to prevent duplicate registration requests.

---

## 7. Summary Diagram

```
Client Request
     │
     ▼
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│  AuthenticationFilter        │  Extracts Bearer token from Authorization header
│  (OncePerRequestFilter)      │  Validates JWT signature + expiry
│                              │  Checks TokenRepository (not revoked/expired)
│                              │  Sets SecurityContext with user authorities
└─────────────────────────────┘
     │
     ▼
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│  SecurityFilterChain         │  URL-level authorization rules
│  (SecurityChain)             │  /auth/** → permitAll
│                              │  /management/** → ADMIN or MANAGER
│                              │  Everything else → authenticated
└─────────────────────────────┘
     │
     ▼
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│  @PreAuthorize               │  Method-level authorization
│  (on controller methods)     │  e.g., hasRole('ADMIN')
└─────────────────────────────┘
     │
     ▼
  Controller → Service → Response
```

This template provides a complete, production-ready security stack with JWT authentication, role-based authorization at both URL and method levels, token revocation, refresh token support, rate limiting, and clear user-facing auth flows.
